coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of a new carbon dot magnetic nanocomposite (CDs@Fe3O4) from Crocus Cancellatus: Characterization and its photocatalytic degradation of fluorescein dye
coredata.dc:creator: Esmail, Lawen Abdulsatar
Jabbar, Hijran Sanaan
Salih, Shalaw Kamal
coredata.dc:description: Using a one-pot hydrothermal treatment of crocus cancellatus for the first time, a simple and very low-cost method for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) with well-distributed size was developed. To obtain better-quality performance of synthesized CDs, the temperature and time of the hydrothermal carbonization reaction of crocus were optimized simply. The as-prepared CDs exhibit exceptional advantages including low toxicity, high fluorescent, and satisfactory chemical stability. Magnetic nanocomposite CDs@Fe3O4 powders were synthesized using the synthesized CDs from crocus cancellatus to study the photocatalytic activity of fluorescein dye under sunlight. The synthesized CDs, Fe3O4, and magnetic nanocomposite were characterized using various techniques including XRD, EDX, SEM, BET, FTIR, VSM and HRTEM analysis. The microstructure images confirmed that CDs were encapsulated in porous magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The results show that the as-synthesized nanocomposite exhibited higher photocatalytic activity under sunlight compared to LED lamp illumination. The fluorescein dye concentration (45 µM) can be decreased by 94.4% within 60 min exposure to sunlight under optimum conditions using 15 mg of nanocomposite. After five recycles, the stability testing demonstrated that 15 mg of nanocomposite had strong photocatalytic activity by removing 75% of the dye.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Crocus cancellatus
Magnetic nanocomposite
Hydrothermal
Photocatalytic
Fluorescein degradation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Health

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Our sincere thanks go out to the employees of the Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University, Erbil, Iraq and Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Health, Koya University, Iraq. No funding was received for this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the preparation of CDs@Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposite and degradation of fluorescein.
(a) HRTEM image (inset figure: particle size distribution) of CDs; (b) SEM images of CDs and (c) EDX analysis of CDs.

(a) HRTEM image (inset: particle size distribution) of Fe3O4 nanoparticles; (b) HRTEM image (inset: particle size distribution) of composite; (c) SAED of Fe3O4 nanoparticles; (d) SAED of composite; (e) SEM image of Fe3O4; and (f) SEM image of composite.
(a) XRD patterns; and (b) UV–visible spectra of as-synthesized CDs, Fe3O4, and the composite (inset image show the high fluorescence behavior of the composite).
(a) FTIR spectra of Fe3O4; (b) FTIR spectra of composite; (c) FTIR spectra of CD; and (d) the magnetic hysteresis loops of Fe3O4.

UV–Visible absorption spectra of photodegradation of the dye using (a) H2O2, H2O2 + CDs, H2O2 + Fe3O4 NPs, H2O2 + CDs@Fe3O4 (0.05 M H2O2, 15 mg Fe3O4, 15 mg CD/Fe3O4); (b) H2O2 + CDs@Fe3O4 in dark, sunlight, LED 5.37 W, or LED 12.0 W for 60 min.

Effect of: (a) Initial dye concentration (b) Composite dosage (the inset of Fig. 7b is the photographs of FL dye solution with concertation of 45 µM and 15 mg composite taken after regular intervals of 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 min of sunlight irradiation); (c) H2O2 concentration; (d) pH on degradation; and (e) CD@Fe2O3 recyclability in the removal of fluorescein dye.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of a new carbon dot magnetic nanocomposite (CDs@Fe3O4) from Crocus Cancellatus: Characterization and its photocatalytic degradation of fluorescein dye

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Health, KOYA University, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Health, KOYA University, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using a one-pot hydrothermal treatment of crocus cancellatus for the first time, a simple and very low-cost method for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) with well-distributed size was developed. To obtain better-quality performance of synthesized CDs, the temperature and time of the hydrothermal carbonization reaction of crocus were optimized simply. The as-prepared CDs exhibit exceptional advantages including low toxicity, high fluorescent, and satisfactory chemical stability. Magnetic nanocomposite CDs@Fe3O4 powders were synthesized using the synthesized CDs from crocus cancellatus to study the photocatalytic activity of fluorescein dye under sunlight. The synthesized CDs, Fe3O4, and magnetic nanocomposite were characterized using various techniques including XRD, EDX, SEM, BET, FTIR, VSM and HRTEM analysis. The microstructure images confirmed that CDs were encapsulated in porous magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The results show that the as-synthesized nanocomposite exhibited higher photocatalytic activity under sunlight compared to LED lamp illumination. The fluorescein dye concentration (45 µM) can be decreased by 94.4% within 60 min exposure to sunlight under optimum conditions using 15 mg of nanocomposite. After five recycles, the stability testing demonstrated that 15 mg of nanocomposite had strong photocatalytic activity by removing 75% of the dye.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon nanomaterials with sizes less than ten nanometers (nm) and are made up of an amorphous or crystalline core with predominant sp2 carbon and an oxidized carbon surface with carboxyl groups [1]. In 2004, CDs, as a new member of the carbon family were found. CDs have a wide spectrum of absorption in visible light, as well as distinctive optical features [2,3]. Carbon dots, a new type of nanomaterial with excellent biocompatibility, low cost, photo-stability, non-toxicity, and environmental friendliness have a lot of applications in biological and environmental systems [1,4]. Although various studies using carbon nanofiber, graphene, and C-60 as electron scavenging agents have been published [5–7]. Furthermore, because of their superior photo-induced electron transport and up-conversion capabilities, CDs outperform alternative carbon-based materials [8,9].

As a major global environmental issue, increasing industrialization releases a substantial number of contaminants into groundwater, lakes, and rivers, resulting in water pollution. It is anticipated that 10–15 percent of organic dyes will be released into the environment, with carcinogenic and mutagenic consequences for people [10]. Therefore, researchers are now investigating strategies for degrading industrial wastewater, particularly organic dyes. Organic pollutants were removed from environmental samples using different adsorbents and photocatalysts, including carbon nanotubes [11], MOFs-Graphene [12], NiTiO3 and ZnTiO3 [13], and CdS@ZnIn2S4 [14]. Degradation of environmental contaminants using photocatalytic technology was considered a promising strategy for many reasons [15,16]. Many nanomaterials have been synthesized and employed as adsorbents and photocatalysts for the separation and degradation of pollutants from the liquid phase [17]. Among the inorganic nanomaterials, iron-based nanoscale materials were the first to be investigated which may be due to their low cost and high decontamination efficiency [18].

Fluorescein (FL) (also called resorcinolphthalein) is an organic dye that appears yellow-green in color. It is an anionic dye. A peak excitation occurs between 465 and 490 nm for fluorescein sodium, which absorbs blue light. The fluorescent properties of FL dye have made it useful in a variety of medical, military, scientific, and industrial applications. For tracing water, FL-Na was the first fluorescent dye to be used. Approximately 20 µg/mL of FL dye is added to some commercial antifreeze preparations [19]. A photo catalyst is a substance that absorbs light energy and converts it to a higher energy level before releasing it to one or more substrates (reagents) to catalyze a specific reaction [20].

However, because the CDs have no polarity, it is difficult to remove them from the aqueous phase after they have been placed in wastewater and degraded. Due to the nanoscale size of these CDs, a costly recyclable technique would be used to separate them from wastewater. Using magnetic particles to connect them is a simple and inexpensive technique to replace traditional separation [21]. Magnetite (Fe3O4) nanoparticles are characterized by low toxicity and biocompatibility. A magnetic field can easily be used to control the mobility of magnetite nanoparticles. Application of magnetic carbon dots includes detection of pathogenic bacteria [22], detection of E. coli bacteria [23], carbamazepine removal [24], and detection of procalcitonin using CDs-encapsulated nano-capsules and magnetic carbon dots as coupled labels [25]. A method was developed for removal of FL dye using sawdust. Factors that affect the removal of the dye from aqueous solutions such as temperature, pH, initial concentration, and time were studied. FL removal decreased from 93.4% to 80.0% with natural pine sawdust and from 96.8% to 81.5% with synthetic pine sawdust by increasing the dye concentration from 2.0 to 10.0 μg/mL [26]. The adsorption of FL dye on wild herb microparticles Juniperus (JH) and Solenostemma argel (Del) Hayne (SH)) was studied to elucidate the changes in adsorption behavior with various parameters. It was determined that the adsorption percentage of JH for FL was as high as 85.5%, which was higher than that of SH (71.9%). The characterization of JH and SH were done using different techniques. In SEM images, JH’s surface showed deep pores ranging in size from 1.90 to 3.50 μm, while SH had smaller pores ranging in size from 130 to 350 μm, which should help it absorb large amounts of dye [27].

In this present investigation, we are discussing the easy, simple, fast, and low-cost synthesis of C-dots from crocus cancellatus through the hydrothermal method and its magnetic composite for photo degradation of fluorescein dye.

FeCl3·6H2O (98%), FeCl2·9H2O (98.5%), NaOH, CH3COOH, Hydrogen peroxide (35% w/w) Merk KGaA Germany, fluorescein dye standard Fluka, the crocus cancelata was obtained from Perbal mountains, Kurdistan, Iraq. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. All experiments were conducted with deionized water.

The UV–visible absorbance spectra were recorded using a (LKB, BIOCHROM 4050/4051) spectrophotometer. PHILIPS' CM120-Netherland transmission electron microscope was used to capture HR-TEM images. FTIR spectra were recorded by the IRAffinity-1 SHIMADZU spectrometer at room temperature. A Perkin Elmer LS-45 fluorescence spectrometer (Buckinghamshire, UK) was used to detect fluorescence measurements. SEM image was obtained by TESCAN MIRA III (Czech). A scanning electron microscope (SEM) is fitted with an EDX system and used for EDX analysis. With 15 kV accelerated voltage, an EDX analysis was performed. On the sample holder, freeze dried carbon dots were attached. TESCAN MIRA III (Czech) SEM coating system machine was used to coat the sample with an extremely thin layer of gold. In order to create signals from the sample, high energy electrons are focused on the sample. Philips (PW, 1730) instrument was used to obtain the XRD pattern.

Crocus was used as a carbon precursor for carbon dots synthesized using a hydrothermal technique with certain modifications, as reported in the literature [28]. One gram of fresh chopped crocus was added to 40 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 30 min in a conventional synthesis. After that, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oven for 8 h at 180 °C. Finally, the suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min before passing through a 0.2 µm micro-syringe filter.

Syntheses of CDs@Fe3O4 were done via co-precipitation reaction according to the reported literature [29] with some modifications. In the procedure, 2.2 g FeCl3·6H2O and 1.14 g FeCl2·4H2O were added in a 250 mL beaker and the reaction was carried out in air nitrogen at 80 °C for one hour with continual stirring. Aqueous solution was added to the reaction beaker containing 25 mL of as-synthesized CDs obtained at the final step of CDs preparation (section 2.3), and the reaction was continued for 30 min. Then, drop by drop, 40 mL of 3 M NaOH was added. The reaction was kept at 80 °C for 2 h with constant stirring. Finally, the black precipitate was collected, decanted with the aid of an external magnet, washed several times with deionized water, and dried under vacuum at ambient temperature. The same process was used to make bare Fe3O4 NPs without the use of carbon dots. Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation of CDs@Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposite and degradation of fluorescein.

To study the photo degradation of the fluorescein solutions, photocatalytic tests were conducted using two visible lamps LED (5.37 W), LED (12.0 W), and sunlight. 15 mg of the composite that used as catalyst was added to 50 mL of FL solution (60 µM) and H2O2 (0.03 M). To increase dye adsorption on the surface of the photocatalyst, the mixture was left in the dark for 30 min. The dye was then degraded by exposing the mixture to LED lamp light. After degradation process, each sample was subjected to centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min (mod S-549)). After that, a 0.2 μm filter membrane was used to filter out the photocatalyst nanoparticles for analysis, and a UV–visible spectrophotometer was used to quantify the quantities of fluorescein in the liquid supernatant (λmax for FL 490 nm) [30]. Using Eq (1), it was possible to calculate the dyes degradation efficiency (η):
(1)
η =
C o
-
C t
C o
× 100 %
where Co and Ct are the dye concentrations at the initial and specified irradiation times, respectively. The effects of concentrations of the dye, the dosage of the magnetic nanocomposite, pH, and H2O2 concentration were optimized by chaining one variable while keeping the rest of the parameters.

The general morphologies for the CDs, Fe3O4 and CDs@Fe3O4 composite were investigated by SEM and HR-TEM images, and the size distribution was estimated using ImageJ software. Typical HRTEM and SEM images of CD are shown in Fig. 2 a and b, respectively. Fig. 2a depicts a typical HRTEM image of as-synthesized C-dots, demonstrating their small size and tiny ball-shaped structure. The corresponding size histogram with CDs size distribution contained within 0.9–1.8 nm is shown in the inset of Fig. 2 a. According to the HRTEM image, the average size of CDs was 1.3 nm [31,32].

An EDX analysis determined the elemental composition of CDs, and the results revealed C (50.37%), O (41.66%), Na (0.47%), Mg (0.51%), Al (1.41%), Si (0.42%), P (1.67%), S (0.63%), Cl (0.54%) and K (2.33%) as in Fig. 2 c. The elemental composition of the synthesized CDs indicates that they were formed mostly from C and O atoms. A minor amount of Mg, Al, Si, Na, Cl, S, P, and K was also found due to the use of crocus cancellatus which may contain these elements.

The HRTEM image for Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 3
a), is evident of the dense population of formed nanoparticles with most particles acquiring a spherical shape. The distribution of particles in the range of 9 – 26 nm is depicted in the histogram inset of Fig. 3 a. The average size of Fe3O4 nanoparticles in this case is estimated to be 16 nm. The SAED pattern is clearly shown as in Fig. 3 b confirming the polycrystalline nature of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Also, the SEM image (Fig. 3 c) shows that the morphology of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles is roughly spherical in shape [33].

Fig. 3 d shows a representative HRTEM image of magnetic nanocomposites. Interestingly, it shows that the encapsulated CDs in Fe3O4 porous. This can be attributed to the agglomeration and uniform distribution of Fe3O4 on the CD surface. The size of composites as estimated from the corresponding HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 3 d) was in the range of 12–23 nm. The average size of composite is estimated to be 17.01 nm [34]. The SAED images of the magnetic nanocomposites sample (Fig. 3 e) clearly show that except for the ring pattern arising from the spherical Fe3O4, there are the spots pattern arising from the CD can be found, further suggesting that CD-Fe3O4 hybrids were effectively obtained [35]. In addition, the N2 adsorption and desorption experiment indicated that the composite featured a micropore volume of 0.2353 cm3/g and BET surface area of 30.605 m2/g, in comparison to 0.2525 cm3/g and 43.561 m2/g for Fe3O4, respectively, suggesting the successful encapsulation of carbon dots in Fe3O4. Also similar results were obtained by Jafari et al. [36]. SEM image shows the morphology of the CD@Fe3O4 structures (Fig. 3 f). The NPs formed tend to agglomerate in addition to having a spherical geometry. As a result of this formation, it can be concluded that the surface energy of NPs is relatively high [37].

The compositional characteristics and crystalline phases of CD and its composite were further analyzed using XRD measurements. The Fe3O4, C-dots, and composite XRD patterns are shown in Fig. 4
a. The XRD pattern for C-dots shows a broad diffraction peak at about 2θ = 23 ° (JCPDS card No. 00-050-0926), indicating the amorphous nature of the as-synthesized carbon dots [38]. For Fe3O4 the peaks of 29.6615°, 35.1196°, 42.4858°, 53.1510b°, 56.6796°, 62.2855°, 70.8114°, and 73.8213° confirmed Fe3O4 structure (JCPDS cards No.98-001-7260). The XRD pattern of the magnetic nanocomposite shows that the characteristic peaks of CD remain unchanged, with new diffraction peaks for Fe3O4, at 18.2561°, 30.0443°, 31.6842°, 35.3731°, 37.0437°, 43.2099°, 45.3882°, 53.5233°, 57.0890°, 62.6801°, 71.1852°, 74.1645°, 75.3232°, (JCPDS cards No 98-011-1282, 00-046-0943) These observations well indicate the formation of a composite of Fe3O4 and CDs [39].

UV–Visible absorption measurements can be used to evaluate the linear optical absorption behavior of CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the UV–Visible spectrum of CDs has produced two different peaks at 280 and 224 nm which is similar to the result obtained by Alarfaj et al. [40]. The π-π* transition of aromatic CC and CC bonds which originated from the aromatic π system could be responsible for the peak at 224 nm [41]. Due to the successful oxidation of carbon-based nanoparticles, a peak at 280 nm might be attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO groups. Under ultraviolet (365 nm) illumination, a very bright blue luminescence was seen (as shown in the inset of Fig. 4 b).

The interactions between CDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles is confirmed by FTIR spectra. Fig. 5
a, b, and c show the spectra for Fe3O4 NPs, composite, and CD, respectively. The FTIR spectra of Fe3O4 NPs show that the broad peaks around 3437.15 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and absorption peak at 1508.33 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of OH groups of the adsorbed water, respectively [42]. The absorption peak at 567.07 cm−1 could be identified as the stretching vibration of the FeO band. The FTIR spectra of magnetic nanocomposite shows the broad band in the region of 3649.32 cm−1 aroused by stretching vibrations of OH, the band at 2358.94 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CH bonds. The band at 1749.44 cm−1 indicates the existence of COO. The absorptions peaks due to the OH bending vibration observed at 1508.33 cm−1 and (CO) vibrations are observed around 1168.86 cm−1 [43]. The absorption at 580.75 cm−1 is observed, which is due to Fe-O stretching vibration in Fe3O4. This result led us to believe that the CDs are bound to the Fe3O4 particles through, most probably, FeO chemical bonds. Other novel bands at 1338.60 and 1647.21 cm−1 might be explained by the formation of FeO bonds between the carboxyl groups in CDs and the iron atoms in Fe3O4 nanoparticles. This demonstrates that CDs and Fe3O4 can coexist well in hybrid composites [34]. An important property of a recyclable catalyst is its magnetic properties. Fig. 5 d illustrates the magnetic hysteresis of the nanocomposite under an applied magnetic field at room temperature. The catalyst has a saturation magnetization (Ms) of 66.52834 emu g -1, showing that the composite as-prepared is a strong magnetic substance that can readily be separated by a magnetic field [44].

The photo degradation of fluorescein dye was carried out in CDs@Fe3O4 composite using 45 μM FL dye. The controlled experimental results suggest that using (0.05 M H2O2) and 15 mg of CDs@Fe3O4 composite is important and leads to enhancement of photo degradation of the dye about 94% under solar irradiation within 60 min. While, using H2O2, H2O2 + Fe3O4 and H2O2 + CDs NPs leads to degradation percentages 10, 25, and 45%, respectively (Fig. 6
a). These results also confirm the synergistic effect between Fe3O4 NPs and CD, which could result in an increase in FL dye removal by CD@Fe3O4. In addition, the degradation process was done using H2O2 + CDs@Fe3O4 in the dark, sunlight, LED (5.37 W), and LED (12.0 W). FL dye in the dark did not show any measurable degradation. As shown in Fig. 6 b, degradation of FL dye under sunlight gives best results.

Different concentrations (45, 60, 75, 90 µM) of 50 mL FL solution were utilized at a contact time of 60 min, and a 15 mg adsorbent dose. Fig. 7
a depicts the effect of varying concentrations on degradation. The dye decolorization efficiency of the composite was dependent on the initial concentration of FL dye in that the maximum degradation took place at (45 µM) which increase from 76.6 to 94.3% with decreasing dye concentration. This could be because dye molecules increase in number, but OH . molecules remain constant. With the dye's initial concentration increasing, more FL dye molecules are adsorbed onto the CDs@Fe3O4 composite's surface, leading to a decrease in the oxidation process [44,45].

Fig. 7 b represents the results of an investigation of the effect of composite dosage on FL degradation. When the dosage of the composite increased from 10 to 15 and 20 mg, (the inset is the photographs of FL dye solution with concertation of (45 µM) and 20 mg composite taken after regular intervals of 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 min of sun light irradiation) the degradation efficiency of FL increased from 82.5 % to 93.0 % and 94.4 % within 60 min, respectively. This study showed that increasing nano-composite dosage could give more active sites and expedite hydrogen peroxide decomposition to produce more OH. radicals resulting in FL dye degradation [46,47]. It should be noted that increasing the composite dosage from 15 to 20 mg enhanced FL decomposition efficiency only a little. A slight improvement in catalytic activity was achieved by agglomerating nanosized composites (20 mg) [48].

The effect of hydrogen peroxide concentration on FL degradation is shown in Fig. 7 c. With a dosage of 0.03 M of peroxide the degradation efficiency of FL can reach 83% at 60 min, while the removal efficiencies of FL dye are 94.4 % and 86.0 % with the hydrogen peroxide concentrations of 0.05 and 0.07 M, respectively. The degradation rate decreased as the hydrogen peroxide dosage was increased from 0.05 to 0.07 M, which could be owing to excess H2O2 scavenging OH radicals [49]. In comparison to OH radicals, the radicals of HO2 generated here are the less reactive radicals, as shown by reaction (2).
(2) OH
+ H2O2 → HO2
. + H2O

As a result of low concentrations of H₂O₂, the formation of the ·OH radical is low, leading to inefficient degradation. Similarly, when hydrogen peroxide is used in high concentrations, the ·OH radicals produced react with the excess amounts of H₂O₂ to produce H₂O and ·OOH radical, and consequently, reduce degradation rates [50].

The effect of varying pH (3.0, 7.0, and 11.0) on the degradation of dye was investigated with a catalyst dosage of 15 mg, initial dye concentration of (45 µM), and contact time of 60 min as shown in Fig. 7 d. The pH of the solution was adjusted using 0.1 M HCl/NaOH. As the pH decreased from 11 to 3 the degradation efficiency increased continuously. After 60 min photo reaction, the percent of degradation reached 95 at pH 3 while at pH 7 it decreased to 84 and 50 at pH 11 (Fig. 7 d). This behavior may be explained by the electrostatic interaction, where at low pH the fluorescein has positively charged which promotes higher adsorption in the surface of the catalyst.

To verify the reuse possibility of the catalyst, five cycles of photo-degradation of 45 µM FL were carried out. Magnetic decantation was used to collect the magnetic nanocomposite, which was subsequently washed with distilled water. An 80 ℃ oven was used to dry the composite. It was re-used for further decomposition. The photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite decreased with the increasing number of cycles. The efficiency of photo-degradation of FL decreased from 94.3 to 75%. High photocatalytic activity was maintained by the composite photocatalyst, and the removal rate of FL on the composite was 75% after 5 cycles as shown in Fig. 7 e. As a result, the composite was extremely stable in sunlight.

Combining CDs with Fe3O4 is responsible for the superior photo-catalytic performance of CD@Fe3O4 composite. CDs and Fe3O4 act as electron reservoirs by preventing charge recombination to generate photoreactive sites by separating photogenerated electron-hole pairs. As soon as the electron–hole pair generates when energy is supplied higher or equal to the photocatalyst band gap energy. These energy levels are beneficial for transferring electrons from the CD conduction band into the closely located charge transfer states of Fe3O4. In order to effectively separate the photo-generated electron-hole pairs, charge recombination will be hindered in order to produce photoreactive sites. This combinational effect of Fe3O4 and CD results in the superior photocatalytic activity of CD/ Fe3O4. Here is a summary of these reactions:
(3) CD@Fe3O4 + hν → CD@Fe3O4 (e‾ + h+ )
(4) h+ + H2O → OH
+ H+
(5) h+ + OH ‾ → OH
(6) e‾ + O2 → O2 ‾
(7) H2O2 + e‾ → OH
(8) OH. + dye → Degradation products

In conclusion, a low-cost synthesis route for fabricating highly fluorescent carbon dots using crocus cancellatus as the precursor for the first time was described with quantum yield of 54.7%, as well as a simple approach for the synthesis of Fe3O4/CDs composite through co-precipitation, and its application to FL dye degradation from aqueous solutions. As a recyclable sunlight-driven photocatalyst, magnetic CD@Fe3O4 nanocomposites were effectively produced. The degradation efficiencies of dye were 94.4% within 60 min sunlight exposure time under optimum conditions using 45 µM of FL dye and 15 mg of nanocomposite. The removal efficiency of FL by the composite increased as the dosage of the composite was increased and decreased as the FL solution initial concentration and pH increased. The stability test demonstrates that 15 mg of CD@Fe3O4 had strong photocatalytic activity by removing 75% of the dye after five recycles.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Our sincere thanks go out to the employees of the Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University, Erbil, Iraq and Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Health, Koya University, Iraq.

ce:section.ce:para: No funding was received for this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of carbon nanodots from single chain polymeric nanoparticles and theoretical investigation of the photoluminescence mechanism
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Zhu, S. Sun, Y. Wang, S. Deng, G. Qian, M. Wang, A. Hu, Preparation of carbon nanodots from single chain polymeric nanoparticles and theoretical investigation of the photoluminescence mechanism, Journal of Materials Chemistry C, 1 (2013) 580-586. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1039/C2TC00140C.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescent carbon nanodots: synthesis, physicochemical properties and analytical applications
Mater. Today

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Roy, P.-C. Chen, A.P. Periasamy, Y.-N. Chen, H.-T. Chang, Photoluminescent carbon nanodots: synthesis, physicochemical properties and analytical applications, Materials Today, 18 (2015) 447-458. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mattod.2015.04.005.
